title: Peppermint and activated charcoal soap
subtitle: A black cold-process bar with peppermint essential oil
excerpt: A cold-process bar soap coloured black with activated charcoal and scented with peppermint essential oil. The charcoal gives the bar a visually striking appearance and adsorbs skin-surface oils; the peppermint provides a cooling, refreshing lather. A bar for oily skin types.
sourceNotes: Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), Ch. 5, hard-bar oil blends. Kevin Dunn, Caveman Chemistry (2003), activated charcoal adsorption in cosmetics. Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), p. 381, peppermint dermal limit and cat toxicity note. SoapCalc SAP-value tables (v2024): olive 0.135, coconut 0.190, castor 0.128.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles to 2 kg. Pre-blend the charcoal with a small amount of olive oil before adding to the batter, which prevents uneven black streaks.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Active work is 90 minutes. Cure 4-6 weeks.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 2 years in a cool dark cupboard once cured. Discard if DOS (orange flecks) or a sharp paint-like smell develops.

glossaryTerms.definition: The percentage of oil left unsaponified. At 5% superfat this bar has extra oil for skin feel.
The point where batter thickens to warm-custard consistency. The charcoal makes tracing hard to see by colour change; watch for the texture change instead.
The 4-6 weeks after unmoulding during which water evaporates and the bar hardens.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Twenty-four hours in. The loaf is firm. Unmould onto a greaseproof-lined board and cut into 130 g bars. The bars will be very dark grey to black from the charcoal. Stand on a wire rack with air space around all faces.

projectSchedule.title: Cure test
projectSchedule.body: Four weeks in. Slice a sliver, wet it, check the lather. The lather should foam white despite the black bar. Turn all bars 90 degrees.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: Six weeks in. The cure is complete. Store in a paper-lined box with the lid ajar.

content.text: A peppermint and activated charcoal bar soap. The charcoal gives the bar its black colour and acts as an adsorbent in the lather, binding to excess sebum and surface debris. Peppermint essential oil at 2% of the oil weight gives a sharp, cooling scent. The oil blend is olive, coconut, and castor at the same ratios as a standard household cold-process bar; the charcoal and peppermint are the variables.

Peppermint essential oil is toxic to cats, dogs, and small pets; keep the oil and the freshly made bars away from pets during the session. Peppermint should not be used on children under 3 years old. Pregnant readers in the first trimester should substitute lavender or omit the essential oil entirely.
: (600 × 0.135) + (300 × 0.190) + (100 × 0.128) = 150.8 g at 0%. At 5%: 143.3 g. Working weight: 143 g NaOH. Water at 33%: 330 g distilled water.

items.prepNote: refined olive oil. 60% of the oil weight.
76°F-melt-point coconut oil. 30% of the oil weight.
cold-pressed castor oil. 10% of the oil weight.
distilled or deionised. 33% of the oil weight.
99% pure cosmetic-grade NaOH. 5% superfat. 0.1 g precision.
food-grade or cosmetic-grade activated charcoal powder. 1.5% of the oil weight. Pre-blend with 30 ml of the olive oil before adding to the batter at trace to prevent dry clumps.
Mentha piperita peppermint essential oil. 2% of the oil weight. Add at trace.

content.text: Weigh 15 g of activated charcoal powder into a small cup. Add 30 ml of the measured olive oil and stir to a smooth paste, no dry lumps. Set aside; this paste goes in at trace.

Weigh 330 g distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Add 143 g NaOH to the water in a steady stream, stirring with a stainless-steel spoon. Never the reverse. The pitcher heats immediately and fumes briefly; do not lean over it. Set aside to cool to 38-42°C.

Weigh the remaining olive oil (570 g), coconut oil, and castor oil into a saucepan and melt over a low heat until the coconut oil is fully liquid. Check the temperature with the probe; both phases need to be in the 38-42°C window. Pour the lye into the oils and pulse the stick blender in 10-second bursts.
Add charcoal paste and peppermint at trace, pour

, note that the batter will be opaque cream-white and trace is visible by texture rather than colour change. Spoon in the charcoal paste and fold through with 20 strokes until the batter turns uniformly dark grey-black, no white streaks. Add the 20 ml of peppermint essential oil and fold through quickly. Pour into the silicone mould. Tap to settle. Cover with a tea towel and leave undisturbed for 24-48 hours.
Unmould after 24-48 hours and cut into bars. The bars will be dark grey to black; the lather foams white. Stand on a wire rack.

Patch-test on the inside forearm for 24-48 hours. Peppermint essential oil can cause a tingling or mild burning sensation on sensitive skin, especially on the face. If this bar is for facial use, consider reducing the peppermint to 1% (10 ml) for the first batch.

Two years in a cool dark cupboard. The charcoal is inert and does not affect the shelf life. Keep away from pets during and after the make session: peppermint essential oil is toxic to cats and dogs even in the cured bar if the animal licks it. Store bars in a closed box well out of reach.
